So you have these examples in there, but these are all out of direct mail packages. So and the picture is one thing. So here's a picture and you have it in your book. It's on page. I don't know. It's in there. 103, 104, 105. Really what's written on the back is as important as the picture. Now the funniest one though, you will think you'll giggle because you laughed before. So here's a picture of Eileen, right? Now the back of it, so this is in a direct mail piece. So the back says, it may be silly to send you my picture, but I wanted you to know I am a real person who truly wants to help you make money part-time, see my letter. Anyway, I hope to meet you in the near future, Eileen. Now, you and I know this does not prove you're a real person. It does actually happen to be a picture of Eileen, but it doesn't prove it. It could be anybody, right? But, and there's no sales copy here, but this is really, really important. Here, the copy on this, so here's the picture, right? So the copy is more of Gossel, Kansas, which by the way, Gossel makes sure it and look like a thriving metropolis. Here's the more incorporated headquarters at the bottom on the right. At the bottom of far left is our local community center and public library that my wife, Eileen, helped build. At the top of the photo, you can see part of our high school football field. This is where most of us gather on Friday night during the football season. Now, there is not a single word there that is sales related. It's all about the place, okay? Because if you live in Gossel, Kansas, you are more trustworthy than if you live in Phoenix, Arizona or Cleveland, Ohio or New York, New York or Los Angeles to most people and particularly to the market that they are targeting, which is 55, 60 and up demographic, married. So we, again, core opportunity marketers get this very well. The Welcome to Gossel, Kansas letter that's in your book is an absolute classic. They get this very well in part because what they are selling is so inherently untrustworthy and hard to get people to believe in. So they got to get these things right. Most other info marketers don't get how important these things are. So for example, do they want to know you're a real person? Yes, they do. Do they want to think if they're in your town that they can drop by your office and there's really an office there and there's real people there and they can come in and everybody will be hanging out? Yes, they do. These things are important. So place. Third is affinity, okay? So opportunity marketers create trust with people by affinity. Sometimes they use that tactically as well. So if you're a fighter pilot, you might seek out other fighter pilots in order to do your marketing too. That gives you direct affinity. But the affinity that is most effective is really tone, conversational tone with your audience, with your market and shared general background and experience. So if you grew up in a small town and now you live in a small town and you're all about small town and you're going to do direct mail, for example, the smartest thing you can do is mail to people who live in small towns and not mail to people who live in big cities. So there's affinity and there's other examples of affinity in your book. Last is being disarming. Now this is particularly interesting, I think. If I had time, I'd show you. So homework assignment, not easy to get, by the way, not hard to get, I don't think. So if you go to, where'd you go to get Turner, Turner, Turner? Is it? GlennWTurner.com. Okay, so if you go, and we'll talk about Turner a little later, but just make a note now so I don't forget. So if you go to GlennWTurner.com and you get a copy of the Challenge to America speech, the challenge, which I'm not going to show you, because the DVDs are all, they're not produced well. So you take your chances just getting the DVD, whether it'll play all the way through, to be honest with you, but it's worth the struggle because you will never see a better example of disarmingness to relax and take away resistance from an audience than this guy doing this speech. So this was the number one recruiting speech, asking people to fork over five thousand bucks, a number of years ago when five thousand dollars was real money, in order to get into a business they couldn't possibly believe in. And most of them, by the time this speech was recorded, they were being drugged to the meetings and the news media was full of what a satanic criminal he was. So the sales environment was, you know, not beneficial. And so these, somebody, so they're all like, you're not getting my wallet kind of audience. And the disarming nature of what he did, as you watch it, you will be able to feel yourself relaxed and be drawn in and not feel that this person is any threat to you whatsoever. So here's the point about every pitch, right? Every pitch, repair, improvement, or opportunity. But so every pitch is a threat to them on several levels, right? It's a threat like we talked about this morning to the conscious mind and its portfolio of beliefs. It's also a threat because it's going to ask them to spend money, right? Everybody knows in advance, as soon as they detect it's a pitch, they know that at the end of the pitch, we're going to ask them to spend money. And nobody wants to part with money, period. And so all the defenses start to go up. So disarming copy, whether delivered from the front of the room or one-on-one, in print, on TV, whatever.
